chunks.title: College of Engineering Industrial and Systems Engineering ISEN 616 Syllabus
chunks.content:
College of Engineering
Industrial and Systems Engineering
ISEN 616 Syllabus
Section 600 (39775)
Design and Analysis of Industrial Experiments
Spring 2026 - College Station

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Design and Analysis of Industrial Experiments. (3-0). Credit 3. Fundamental theory, concepts and procedures required for industrial experimental design, statistical data analysis, and model building, with emphasis on engineering formulations and applications; one-factor experiments with and without restrictions on randomization, treatment comparison procedures, Latin and other squares, factorial experiments, full and fractional two-level factorial experiments, blocking in factorial designs, response surface methodologies and introduction to Taguchi methods. Prerequisite: STAT 212 or STAT 601.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): STAT 212 or STAT 601.

Participants will be assumed to have basic knowledge in statistics, including parameter estimation, hypothesis testing and confidence intervals. Read the pre-course reading materials to refresh your knowledge.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Experiments: Planning, Analysis and Optimization, 3nd Edition (by C. F. Jeff Wu and Mike Hamada), 2021, John Wiley.
Notes:
Thanks to the support of Texas A&M University Libraries, you can access the Wu-Hamada textbook for free:
http://proxy.library.tamu.edu/login?url=https://app.knovel.com/hotlink/toc/id:kpEPAOE002/experiments-planning-analysis?kpromoter=marc

This material Is: Recommended
R
Notes:
R statistical software package will be used for the example discussed in class, home assignments and examination. Please install the R software on your laptop from http://cran.r-project.org. You will use this software for homework assignments.

This material Is: Optional
Design and Analysis of Experiments with R. (by Lawson, John), 2014. CRC press.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
• Grades assigned are A for 90%-100%, B for 80%–89.9%, C for 70%–79.9%, D for 60%-69.9% and F for less than 60%.
• Any disagreements regarding a grade received on any graded material must be discussed within two business days of the return of the graded material. No grade will be changed beyond the two business days limit.
• Grades will be maintained in the gradebook within Canvas.
• Grading components:

Homework – 10%
Exam 1 – 20%
Exam 2 -20%
Final Exam – 30%
Group Project – 20%

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Homework will be released on Canvas and is due on the designated. Late homework will be docked by 15% per every day it is late. No extensions will be made if the due date is your job interview day/exam day/holiday/etc. For homework before two exams (I will remind you which ones are those), no late homework will be accepted. If you would like to have your homework or exam re-graded, you have to do so within 10 days from the time when the homework/exam is returned to you.

Description of the project tasks will be announced on Canvas. Project report is due on the designated date. Late project report will be docked by 15% per every day it is late

chunks.title: Course Contents/Topics
chunks.content:
1. Basic principles and introduction to regression analysis
2. Experiments with a single factor, analysis of variance
3. Experiments with more than one factor, blocking, analysis of variance
4. Factorial experiments at two levels, comparison with “one-factor-at-a-time” plans, analysis of location and dispersion, choice of optimal blocking schemes
5. Fractional factorial experiments at two levels, maximum resolution and minimum aberration for choosing optimal designs, choice of optimal blocking schemes
6. Orthogonal arrays
7. Introduction to fractional factorial experiments at three levels
8. Variation reduction, robust parameter design for product and process improvement

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
• Week 1 Topic 1
• Week 2 Topic 2
• Weeks 4-5 Topic 3
• Weeks 6-8 Topic 4
• Week 9 Spring break
• Weeks 10-11 Topic 5
• Week 12 Topic 6
• Week 13-14 Topic 7
• Week 15 Topic 8

chunks.title: EXAM SCHEDULE
chunks.content:
Exam 1: Friday February 13, in class.
Exam 2: Friday April 17, in class.
Final Exam: Thursday April 30, 10:00 a.m. - 12:00 p.m. (noon).

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving appropriate credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles. AI text generators (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.) should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to:

• Creating or revising drafts
• Editing your work
• Reviewing a peer's work

This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are acceptable.

Such use of AI text generators in this manner could be considered plagiarism and cheating according to Student Rule 20. More information may also be found at https://aggiehonor.tamu.edu or you may contact your instructor if you have questions.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content: Class attendance will be taken for quality assessment purpose.

chunks.title: Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of more than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed upon by the student and instructor” (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.1).

"The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up work missed because of an unexcused absence” (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2).

Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and Student Conduct Code. (See Student Rule 24.)

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical factors influencing a student's academic success and overall wellbeing. Students are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care practices by utilizing the resources and services available through University Health Services. The TELUS Health Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple languages anytime, anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency support at 988 or 988lifeline.org.

Texas A&M College Station
Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services at 979.458.4584. Call 911 or visit your nearest emergency room if you are currently experiencing a life-threatening situation or if your safety is at risk. 24-hour emergency help is also available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (988) or at 988lifeline.org.
